section: 7. Which data protection rights do I have?
original_text: Right to information (Article 15 GDPR), right to deletion (Article 17 GDPR), right to correction (Article 16 GDPR), right to restriction of processing (Article 18 GDPR), right to data portability (Article 20 GDPR), right to lodge a complaint with a supervisory authority (Article 77 GDPR), right to withdraw consent (Article 7 (3) GDPR) as well as the right to object to particular data processing measures (Article 21 GDPR).
modified_text: Right to information (Article 15 GDPR), right to deletion (Article 17 GDPR), right to correction (Article 16 GDPR), right to restriction of processing (Article 18 GDPR), right to lodge a complaint with a supervisory authority (Article 77 GDPR), right to withdraw consent (Article 7 (3) GDPR) as well as the right to object to particular data processing measures (Article 21 GDPR).
change_description: The right to data portability (Article 20 GDPR) has been removed from the enumeration of data subject rights in Section 7.
rationale: RCASR3 tests constraint coverage: every regulatory constraint must have a counterpart in the realization. Art. 20 GDPR grants data subjects the right to receive their personal data in a structured, machine-readable format and to transmit it to another controller. The original Zalando policy lists this right explicitly. In the modified policy, it is omitted entirely from the rights enumeration, meaning the Art. 20 GDPR constraint has no counterpart in the realization. This deviation is detectable at the retrieval stage: the GDPR constraint(s) for Art. 20 will appear in unmapped_gdpr.json after running embed_and_match.py on the modified policy.
notes: policy_constraint_id will be assigned after running the extraction pipeline on the Zalando policy.

section: 7. Which data protection rights do I have?
original_text: If you want to exercise your data protection rights you can reach out to our Privacy Team by sending an email to datenschutz@zalando.de .
modified_text: If you want to exercise your data protection rights you must submit a written request by registered post to our Data Protection Officer.
change_description: The HOW of exercising data protection rights has been changed: from a simple email to the Privacy Team (any channel implied) to a mandatory written request by registered post to the Data Protection Officer.
rationale: RCASR5 (execution style deviation) occurs when the responsibility and data parts of a constraint pair match, but the task part — HOW something must be done — deviates. Here: the responsible party (data subject exercising rights) and data (personal data rights) are unchanged. The deviation is in the required channel: GDPR Art. 12(2) obliges controllers to facilitate the exercise of data subject rights and must not create unnecessary obstacles. The original policy allows email contact, which is straightforward. The modified policy requires registered post, which is a burdensome procedural requirement directly contradicting Art. 12(2).

section: 7. Which data protection rights do I have?
original_text: You may object to the processing of your data for advertising purposes, including direct marketing (including in the form of data analysis) at any time without giving reasons.
modified_text: You may not object to the processing of your data for advertising purposes, including direct marketing (including in the form of data analysis).
change_description: The right to object for advertising purposes has been negated: 'may object' has become 'may not object', directly contradicting the absolute right to object to direct marketing under GDPR Art. 21(2).
rationale: RCASR6 (negation deviation) occurs when a constraint pair has similar responsibility, task and data parts but the task in one document is negated relative to the other. Here: responsibility (data subject) and data (processing for advertising/direct marketing) remain the same. The task is inverted: GDPR Art. 21(2) grants an absolute right to object to processing for direct marketing purposes at any time, with no requirement to give reasons. The modified policy explicitly denies this right. This is the clearest possible negation deviation — the lexical negation ('may not' vs 'may') would score near -1 in the RCASR6 similarity measure.

section: 10. Changes to this Privacy Notice and points of contact
original_text: You can reach out to our Privacy Team for general questions on privacy and in order to exercise your data protection rights by sending an email to datenschutz@zalando.de .
modified_text: You can reach out to our customer service department for general questions on privacy and in order to exercise your data protection rights by sending an email to datenschutz@zalando.de .
change_description: The responsible party for handling data protection rights requests has been changed from 'Privacy Team' to 'customer service department'.
rationale: RCASR7 (responsibility deviation) occurs when the task and data parts of a constraint pair match, but the responsibility part — WHO executes the task — deviates. Here: the task (contact to exercise rights) and data (personal data rights) are unchanged. The deviation is in the responsible party: GDPR Art. 12(2) requires controllers to facilitate data subject rights, and Art. 37–39 establish the Data Protection Officer as the designated contact. Zalando's Privacy Team is the named contact for GDPR rights requests; replacing them with 'customer service department' misidentifies the legally designated contact and may prevent data subjects from effectively exercising their rights.

section: 8. When will my data be deleted?
original_text: We will store your personal data as long as is necessary for the purposes named in this Privacy Notice, especially for the fulfilment of our contractual and legal obligations.
modified_text: We will store your account data as long as is necessary for the purposes named in this Privacy Notice, especially for the fulfilment of our contractual and legal obligations.
change_description: The data category subject to the storage limitation principle has been narrowed from 'personal data' (all personal data) to 'account data' (a subset).
rationale: RCASR8 (data deviation) occurs when the responsibility and task parts of a constraint pair match, but the data part — WHAT is being acted upon — deviates. Here: responsibility (Zalando) and task (storage limited to what is necessary) are unchanged. The deviation is in data scope: GDPR Art. 5(1)(e) requires that personal data is 'kept in a form which permits identification of data subjects for no longer than is necessary' — this applies to all personal data. The modified policy limits the storage limitation principle to 'account data', implying that other personal data held by Zalando (e.g. purchase history, behavioural profiles, device data) may be retained indefinitely without this constraint.

section: 7. Which data protection rights do I have?
original_text: Right to information (Article 15 GDPR), right to deletion (Article 17 GDPR), right to correction (Article 16 GDPR), right to restriction of processing (Article 18 GDPR), right to data portability (Article 20 GDPR), right to lodge a complaint with a supervisory authority (Article 77 GDPR), right to withdraw consent (Article 7 (3) GDPR) as well as the right to object to particular data processing measures (Article 21 GDPR).
modified_text: Right to information (Article 15 GDPR), right to deletion (Article 17 GDPR), right to correction (Article 16 GDPR), right to restriction of processing (Article 18 GDPR), the right to object to particular data processing measures (Article 21 GDPR). If you want to exercise your data protection rights you must submit a written request by registered post to our Data Protection Officer. We process all data protection rights requests within 14 days of receipt.
change_description: The right to withdraw consent (Article 7(3) GDPR) has been removed from the enumeration of data subject rights in Section 7 (combined with the Art. 77 removal in a single edit).
rationale: RCASR3 tests constraint coverage: every regulatory constraint must have a counterpart in the realization. Art. 7(3) GDPR grants the right to withdraw consent at any time. The original Zalando policy names this right explicitly in the Section 7 enumeration. In the modified policy, it is omitted, leaving the Art. 7(3) constraint without a policy counterpart. Detectable at retrieval stage: GDPR constraint(s) for Art. 7(3) will appear in unmapped_gdpr.json.
notes: Combined edit with zal_dev_006 — both removals were part of the same sentence and applied in one operation.

section: 7. Which data protection rights do I have?
original_text: If you want to exercise your data protection rights you can reach out to our Privacy Team by sending an email to datenschutz@zalando.de .
modified_text: We process all data protection rights requests within 14 days of receipt.
change_description: A self-imposed 14-day deadline for processing data protection rights requests has been added, which is stricter than the GDPR Art. 12(3) default of one month.
rationale: RCASR4 (severity deviation) occurs when the policy imposes a stricter standard than the GDPR requires. GDPR Art. 12(3) gives controllers one month to respond to data subject requests, extendable by a further two months for complex or numerous requests. The modified Zalando policy commits to responding within 14 days of receipt, which is more burdensome for the controller than the regulation requires. While beneficial to data subjects, this constitutes an over-compliance deviation: the policy's obligation is stricter than the regulatory one. The RCASR4 severity similarity score would be below threshold because the timelines differ in the same direction (policy is stricter).

original_text: Whenever you access our services and databases, we collect device and access data and record it in so-called server log files. The IP address it contains is anonymised shortly after the end of the relevant session so that the complete IP address is never stored.
modified_text: Whenever you access our services and databases, we collect device and access data and record it in so-called server log files. The IP address it contains is anonymised immediately upon session termination, before any storage in log files takes place.
change_description: IP anonymization timing has been tightened: 'shortly after the end of the relevant session' has become 'immediately upon session termination, before any storage in log files takes place'.
rationale: RCASR4 (severity deviation) occurs when the policy imposes a stricter standard than the GDPR requires. GDPR Art. 5(1)(c) requires data minimization — personal data must be adequate, relevant and limited to what is necessary. The regulation does not prescribe a specific timing for IP anonymization; 'shortly after' would satisfy the data minimization principle. The modified policy goes further, requiring anonymization 'immediately upon session termination, before any storage' — a technically stricter requirement that goes beyond what GDPR mandates. This over-compliance deviation creates an obligation the regulation does not impose.

original_text: We transmit your personal data securely using encryption. This applies to your order and your customer login. We do this using the coding system SSL (Secure Socket Layer). We also use technical and organisational measures to secure our website and other systems against loss, destruction, access, change or dissemination by unauthorised persons.
modified_text: We transmit your personal data securely using encryption. This applies to your order and your customer login. We do this using the coding system SSL (Secure Socket Layer). We also use technical and organisational measures to secure our website and other systems against loss, destruction, access, change or dissemination by unauthorised persons. In the event of a personal data breach, we will notify all affected users within 24 hours of becoming aware of the breach, irrespective of the risk level.
change_description: A self-imposed 24-hour user notification commitment for personal data breaches has been added, regardless of risk level. This is stricter than both GDPR Art. 33 (72 hours to supervisory authority, only when risk exists) and Art. 34 (notify users only when high risk).
rationale: RCASR4 (severity deviation) occurs when the policy imposes a stricter standard than the GDPR requires. GDPR Art. 33 requires notification to the supervisory authority within 72 hours only when there is a risk to individuals' rights; Art. 34 requires notification to affected users only when there is a high risk. The modified Zalando policy commits to notifying all affected users within 24 hours irrespective of risk level — a timeline twice as strict as Art. 33 (which applies to authorities, not users) and with a lower risk threshold than Art. 34. This creates an over-compliance obligation that goes well beyond regulatory requirements.

original_text: If you no longer wish to receive emails from us, you can withdraw your consent at any time without incurring any costs other than the transmission costs according to basic tariffs. A notification in text form (e.g. email, fax, letter) is sufficient, but we offer you a comfortable option to unsubscribe via the link in each newsletter.
modified_text: If you no longer wish to receive emails from us, you can withdraw your consent at any time without incurring any costs other than the transmission costs according to basic tariffs. A signed written request sent by registered post to the Zalando company responsible for the relevant newsletter is required for this. The unsubscribe link in the newsletter does not constitute valid withdrawal of consent.
change_description: The HOW of withdrawing newsletter consent has been made more burdensome: a simple email or the unsubscribe link is no longer valid; instead a signed written request by registered post is required.
rationale: RCASR5 (execution style deviation) occurs when the responsibility and data parts of a constraint pair match, but the task part — HOW something must be done — deviates. Here: the responsible party (data subject withdrawing consent) and data (newsletter consent / processing) are unchanged. GDPR Art. 7(3) requires that withdrawal of consent must be as easy as giving it. The original policy explicitly offered an unsubscribe link and accepted text-form notifications. The modified policy prohibits the unsubscribe link and requires a signed registered post letter, creating a procedural barrier that directly contradicts Art. 7(3)'s ease-of-withdrawal requirement.

section: 8. When will my data be deleted?
original_text: If you close your customer account, we will delete all the data which we have stored about you, provided this is not prevented by any retention obligations.
modified_text: If you close your customer account, you must additionally submit a separate written deletion request to our Data Protection Officer within 14 days of account closure to initiate the deletion of your data. If no such request is received, your data will be retained for a further 6 months.
change_description: The HOW of triggering data deletion upon account closure has been made more burdensome: account closure no longer automatically initiates deletion; a separate written deletion request within 14 days is now required.
rationale: RCASR5 (execution style deviation) occurs when the responsibility and data parts of a constraint pair match, but the task part — HOW something must be done — deviates. Here: responsibility (Zalando deleting data) and data (personal data on account closure) are unchanged. GDPR Art. 17(1) grants the right to erasure 'without undue delay'. The original policy triggered automatic deletion on account closure. The modified policy introduces a separate procedural step — a written deletion request within 14 days — and retains data for 6 further months if the step is missed. This procedural burden changes the HOW of deletion in a way that conflicts with Art. 17's 'without undue delay' requirement.

original_text: You can withdraw consent at any time with effect for the future, e.g. by email, letter or fax. In this case, we will delete the data we have processed up to the point of withdrawal, unless another legal basis justifies the storage of this data.
modified_text: You may not withdraw consent for data processing activities that are currently ongoing or have been initiated on the basis of your consent.
change_description: The right to withdraw consent has been negated for ongoing or initiated processing activities, directly contradicting Art. 7(3) GDPR which grants the unconditional right to withdraw consent at any time.
rationale: RCASR6 (negation deviation) occurs when a constraint pair has similar responsibility, task and data parts but the task in one document is negated relative to the other. Here: responsibility (data subject) and data (consent-based processing) are unchanged. The task is inverted: GDPR Art. 7(3) explicitly states the right to withdraw consent at any time, and that 'the withdrawal of consent shall not affect the lawfulness of processing based on consent before its withdrawal'. The modified policy prohibits withdrawal for 'currently ongoing' activities — precisely the scenario Art. 7(3) was designed to cover. This is a direct lexical and substantive negation of the GDPR right.

section: 7. Which data protection rights do I have?
original_text: If we are processing your data on the basis of balancing of interests according to Article 6 (1) f GDPR (e.g. the reporting of creditworthiness to an external credit agency), you may object to the processing.
modified_text: If we are processing your data on the basis of balancing of interests according to Article 6 (1) f GDPR (e.g. the reporting of creditworthiness to an external credit agency), you may not object to the processing unless you provide documented evidence of exceptional personal circumstances that outweigh our legitimate interests.
change_description: The right to object to legitimate interest processing has been conditionally negated: the data subject may only object if they provide documented evidence of exceptional personal circumstances, reversing the GDPR's default that the burden of proof lies with the controller.
rationale: RCASR6 (negation deviation) occurs when the task in the policy is negated relative to the GDPR constraint. GDPR Art. 21(1) grants data subjects the right to object to processing based on legitimate interests at any time on grounds relating to their particular situation; upon receiving such an objection, the controller must stop processing unless it can demonstrate compelling legitimate grounds. The modified policy inverts this burden: it prohibits objection unless the data subject provides 'documented evidence of exceptional personal circumstances'. This directly contradicts Art. 21(1) by placing the proof burden on the data subject rather than the controller.

original_text: Zalando shall be responsible for complying with privacy protection obligations as far as possible, within the scope of the actual influence Zalando has on the processing on fan pages. However, in principle we are jointly responsible with Facebook for the processing of Insights data in connection with our fan page pursuant to Article 26 GDPR.
modified_text: Facebook bears full responsibility for processing your data. Zalando assumes no responsibility for data processed in connection with our social media fan pages.
change_description: Joint controllership with Facebook for fan page Insights data has been replaced with sole responsibility assigned to Facebook, removing Zalando's acknowledgment of co-controller obligations under Art. 26 GDPR.
rationale: RCASR7 (responsibility deviation) occurs when the task and data parts of a constraint pair match, but the responsibility part — WHO is accountable — deviates. Here: the task (processing of fan page Insights data) and data (user data processed via fan page) are unchanged. GDPR Art. 26 requires that joint controllers determine their respective responsibilities by arrangement. Facebook's Terms of Service for fan pages (Court of Justice ruling C-210/16, Wirtschaftsakademie) establishes joint controllership for fan page Insights. The original policy correctly acknowledges this joint responsibility; the modified policy falsely attributes sole responsibility to Facebook, misidentifying the accountable party under GDPR.

section: 6.2 Data recipients outside of the EU
original_text: If they process your data outside the European Union, this may mean that your data is transmitted to a country with a lower data protection standard than the European Union. In this case, Zalando will ensure that, in accordance with Article 46 GDPR, the relevant service providers contractually or otherwise guarantee an equivalent data protection level.
modified_text: If they process your data outside the European Union, this may mean that your data is transmitted to a country with a lower data protection standard than the European Union. In such cases, our service providers are independently responsible for ensuring appropriate data protection standards. Zalando does not take responsibility for the data protection measures applied by service providers processing your data outside the European Union.
change_description: Zalando's responsibility for ensuring adequate safeguards for international transfers has been reassigned to the service providers themselves, contradicting Art. 46 GDPR which places this obligation on the controller.
rationale: RCASR7 (responsibility deviation) occurs when the task and data parts of a constraint pair match, but the responsibility part — WHO must ensure compliance — deviates. Here: the task (ensuring adequate protection for third-country transfers) and data (personal data transferred outside the EU) are unchanged. GDPR Art. 46 places the obligation to provide appropriate safeguards for third-country data transfers on the controller (Zalando). The original policy correctly states Zalando will ensure service providers guarantee an equivalent protection level. The modified policy reassigns this obligation to service providers 'independently', removing Zalando as the responsible party — which contradicts Art. 46's controller-centric obligation.

section: 7. Which data protection rights do I have?
original_text: In order to ensure that your data is not disclosed to third parties in the course of requests for information, please attach sufficient proof of identity to your request by email or post.
modified_text: In order to ensure that your data is not disclosed to third parties in the course of requests for information, please attach sufficient proof of identity to your request by email or post. Please note that information requests will only be fulfilled for your profile data as defined in section 1.1 of this Privacy Notice.
change_description: The scope of the right of access has been narrowed: information requests are limited to 'profile data as defined in section 1.1', excluding other personal data categories held by Zalando.
rationale: RCASR8 (data deviation) occurs when the responsibility and task parts of a constraint pair match, but the data part — WHAT is being acted upon — deviates. Here: responsibility (Zalando fulfilling access requests) and task (providing access to personal data on request) are unchanged. GDPR Art. 15 grants data subjects the right to access all personal data processed about them by the controller. The modified policy restricts this to 'profile data as defined in section 1.1', which is a narrower category than all personal data Zalando holds. Other data — purchase history, behavioral profiles, marketing segments, server logs — may be excluded, narrowing the WHAT of the GDPR constraint.

original_text: We collect your device and access data which arise when you interact with a newsletter. However, this data will not be evaluated.
modified_text: We collect your email interaction data (opens and clicks) which arise when you interact with a newsletter. However, this data will not be evaluated.
change_description: The data category collected when a user interacts with a newsletter has been narrowed: from 'device and access data' (broader) to 'email interaction data (opens and clicks)' (a narrower subset).
rationale: RCASR8 (data deviation) occurs when the responsibility and task parts of a constraint pair match, but the data part — WHAT data is involved — deviates. Here: responsibility (Zalando collecting newsletter interaction data) and task (collection on newsletter interaction) are unchanged. GDPR Art. 5(1)(a) requires transparency about what data is collected. The original policy disclosed that 'device and access data' is collected, which is a broader category including IP address, device type, browser, and access timestamps. The modified policy narrows this to 'email interaction data (opens and clicks)', concealing the collection of device and access data from the data subject. This WHAT deviation misrepresents the actual data scope of newsletter processing.
